region_of_origin: Vietnam (served in Singapore Vietnamese cuisine context)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311349 covers beef shank as the closest parent; no specific heel muscle sub-cut code exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKL covers bovine muscle tissue broadly; no heel-specific sub-cut facet available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226934003 is the closest parent for beef as a substance; no heel muscle-specific code exists.

serving.description: 1 serving of beef heel muscle in pho
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of beef heel muscle in Vietnamese pho is approximately 150 g of braised meat sliced and placed in a bowl of broth with rice noodles.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, shank crosscuts, separable lean only, cooked, simmered — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13360)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Beef heel muscle is the deep flexor muscle of the shank. USDA SR Legacy NDB 13360 (beef shank, separable lean only, simmered) is the closest approved match for a braised/simmered lean beef shank cut. Values adapted from this entry as the heel muscle is anatomically part of the shank complex and has a comparable lean composition when slow-cooked.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of pure protein and fat foods — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure lean beef contains negligible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature confirm that protein- and fat-dominant foods with zero digestible carbohydrate have a GI of effectively 0 (no glycemic response). Per validator convention, glycemic_index is set to 0 and gi_category is 'low'. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (0 × 150) / 100 = 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~71 mg per 100 g) in the lean braised cut itself
hypertension.guidance: Beef heel muscle as a lean braised cut is low in sodium (~71 mg/100 g); individuals with hypertension should monitor total sodium from the accompanying pho broth, which can contribute 800–1200 mg sodium per bowl, and request reduced-sodium broth where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero digestible carbohydrate; high protein supports satiety and glycemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef heel muscle contains zero digestible carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of effectively 0, making it an appropriate protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; portion control of the rice noodles in the accompanying pho is the primary glycemic consideration.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.7 g/100 g) and dietary cholesterol (~93 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While beef heel muscle is a lean cut with moderate saturated fat (2.7 g/100 g), individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100–150 g per meal and balance intake with vegetables and whole grains to stay within saturated fat targets of less than 7% of total energy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef heel muscle (also known as beef shank heel or bap bo in Vietnamese) is a lean, dense cut taken from the deep flexor muscles of the bovine lower leg. It is prized in Vietnamese cuisine, particularly in pho (phở), for its firm yet yielding texture after prolonged slow-cooking or braising. When simmered for several hours in a spiced bone broth flavoured with star anise, cinnamon, ginger, and fish sauce, the collagen-rich connective tissue partially converts to gelatin, giving the meat a succulent, slightly sticky mouthfeel while remaining distinctly meaty. In Singapore, this cut is served at Vietnamese restaurants as part of pho noodle soup dishes, typically sliced and placed atop rice vermicelli noodles in a rich, aromatic broth. Nutritionally, beef heel muscle is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate food providing approximately 199 kcal, 30.9 g protein, and 7.4 g fat per 100 g. It is an excellent source of zinc (7.1 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (2.6 mcg/100 g), iron (3.2 mg/100 g), and selenium (28 mcg/100 g). The glycemic index is effectively zero due to negligible carbohydrate content, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The moderate saturated fat content warrants attention for cardiovascular disease management. Sodium in the meat itself is low, but the pho broth can be high in sodium and should be monitored by individuals with hypertension.
common_user_queries: Is beef heel muscle good for pho?
Is beef heel muscle high in protein?
Can diabetics eat beef heel muscle?
What cut of beef is used in pho?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy NDB 13360 (beef shank, separable lean only, simmered), which is the closest approved match for braised beef heel muscle. The heel muscle is anatomically part of the shank complex and has a comparable lean composition; however, no exact USDA entry for 'beef heel muscle' exists, so confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source — beef heel muscle contains no digestible carbohydrate. sugar_g is confirmed zero for the same reason. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero as this is a pure muscle meat with no plant-derived fibre. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero as beef muscle contains no ascorbic acid. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA source. 2. glycemic_index is set to 0 (effectively zero) as this food contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is 'low' per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 since carbohydrate_g is 0. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this cut. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this cut. 4. Halal status is marked false as this is a beef product served at a Vietnamese restaurant in Singapore; halal certification of the specific restaurant (Viet'Spice) has not been confirmed from an approved source. 5. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 13360 (beef shank crosscuts, separable lean only, cooked, simmered) as the closest approved analogue for braised beef heel muscle.
